title: Token-bucket rate limiter for the auth API
subtitle: PR #482 · auth-service

gate.prompt: In a token-bucket limiter, what does the bucket 'refill rate' control?

options.text: The sustained request rate allowed over time
The maximum burst allowed in a single instant
How long a blocked client must wait before retrying
The total number of clients the service can track

gate.explanation: Refill rate sets the long-run steady-state throughput; bucket capacity sets the burst.

gate.prompt: Why store the bucket state in Redis rather than in process memory?

options.text: Redis is faster than local memory for this
So the limit is shared consistently across all service instances
Redis encrypts the counters automatically

gate.explanation: Per-instance memory would let a client exceed the global limit by hitting different pods.

gate.prompt: What happens when a bucket is empty and a request arrives?

options.text: The request is queued until the next refill tick
The request is rejected with 429 Too Many Requests
The bucket capacity is temporarily doubled

gate.explanation: Empty bucket means no tokens to spend, so the request is refused with 429.

sections.heading: Background
sections.summary: The auth API had no rate limiting, so a single misbehaving client could exhaust database connections for everyone. This PR adds a shared, per-client token-bucket limiter backed by Redis.
sections.body:
Before this change, every request to /auth/login hit the user database directly with no throttle. During the March incident a retry storm from one mobile build opened thousands of connections and starved the pool.

client -> auth-service -> user-db   (no limiter)

The fix introduces a limiter between the handler and the database.

sections.heading: Intuition
sections.summary: Picture a bucket that holds tokens. Each request spends one token; the bucket refills at a fixed rate. Bursts are fine until the bucket runs dry, then requests get a 429.
sections.body:
A token bucket has two knobs: capacity (how big a burst you tolerate) and refill rate (sustained throughput). A client can spend a whole bucket at once, but then must wait for refills.

capacity = 20 tokens
refill   = 5 tokens/sec

[####################] full  -> burst of 20 ok
[                    ] empty -> next request = 429

sections.heading: The code
sections.summary: A new RateLimiter class wraps Redis with an atomic Lua script that refills and decrements in one round trip. The login handler calls allow(clientId) and returns 429 on refusal.
sections.body:
The core is an atomic check-and-decrement so two concurrent requests can't both spend the last token:

result = redis.eval(TOKEN_BUCKET_LUA, key=client_id)
if result.allowed:
    proceed()
else:
    return Response(429, retry_after=result.retry_after)

Doing the refill math inside the Lua script keeps it race-free across instances.

quiz.prompt: Where is the bucket state kept so the limit holds across instances?

options.text: In each pod's local memory
In Redis
In the user database
In a signed cookie on the client

quiz.explanation: Redis gives all instances one shared view of each client's bucket.

quiz.prompt: Why is the refill-and-decrement done in a Lua script?

options.text: To make it atomic so concurrent requests can't double-spend the last token
Because Redis cannot store integers otherwise
To encrypt the token counts
To avoid needing a Redis connection

quiz.explanation: One atomic round trip prevents the check-then-act race.

quiz.prompt: A client sends 20 requests instantly against a full 20-token bucket. What happens?

options.text: All 20 succeed; the bucket is then empty
Only the first is allowed; the rest 429
All 20 are queued and released one per second
The bucket rejects all 20 as a burst

quiz.explanation: Capacity is 20, so a full bucket absorbs a 20-request burst, then sits empty until refill.

quiz.prompt: What status code does a refused request receive?

options.text: 503 Service Unavailable
429 Too Many Requests
403 Forbidden
409 Conflict

quiz.explanation: 429 is the standard 'you are being rate limited' response.

quiz.prompt: Which knob controls sustained throughput rather than burst size?

options.text: Bucket capacity
Refill rate
The Redis key TTL
The Lua script length

quiz.explanation: Refill rate = steady-state rate; capacity = burst allowance.
